issues.title: TypeScript vs JavaScript
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "assertion",
  "rootId": 1,
  "parentId": null
}
-->

TypeScript is strictly better than plain JavaScript for any team with more than 5 engineers.

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "challenge",
  "challengeType": "interrogatory",
  "rootId": 1,
  "parentId": 1,
  "disputeId": 301
}
-->

Does "strictly better" hold even when the team has zero TypeScript experience and a deadline in two weeks?

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "challenge",
  "challengeType": "interrogatory",
  "rootId": 1,
  "parentId": 201,
  "disputeId": 301
}
-->

Can you cite empirical data showing that TypeScript reduces runtime errors more than strong linting rules + JSDoc would?

issues.title: dsp:answer
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "answer",
  "rootId": 1,
  "parentId": 101,
  "disputeId": 301
}
-->

Yes — the upfront ramp-up cost is typically recovered within a sprint because of reduced runtime errors.

issues.title: dsp:answer
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "answer",
  "rootId": 1,
  "parentId": 102,
  "disputeId": 301
}
-->

The 2022 Stripe engineering blog post and the Microsoft internal study both cite ~15% reduction in production bugs after TypeScript migration.

issues.title: Dispute #301 — TypeScript vs JS
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "dispute",
  "challengerId": 1001,
  "challengerLogin": "alice",
  "defenderId": 1002,
  "defenderLogin": "bob",
  "rootPostId": 1,
  "triggerChallengeId": 101
}
-->

issues.title: dsp:assertion
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "assertion",
  "rootId": 2,
  "parentId": null
}
-->

The Great Wall of China is visible to the naked eye from low Earth orbit.

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "challenge",
  "challengeType": "objection",
  "rootId": 2,
  "parentId": 2,
  "disputeId": 302
}
-->

This claim is factually wrong. Every NASA astronaut who has commented on the subject, including Ed Lu and Chris Hadfield, has explicitly stated the wall is too narrow to be seen without optical aids.

issues.title: dsp:answer
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "answer",
  "rootId": 2,
  "parentId": 103,
  "disputeId": 302
}
-->

China's own astronaut Yang Liwei reported in 2003 that he could not see it either — but the wall's length compensates for its narrowness under certain light and atmospheric conditions.

issues.title: Dispute #302 — Great Wall visible from space
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",

}
-->

issues.title: dsp:assertion
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "assertion",
  "rootId": 3,
  "parentId": null
}
-->

Pineapple belongs on pizza. Sweet-savoury combinations are culinarily valid and widely popular.

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "challenge",
  "challengeType": "interrogatory",
  "rootId": 3,
  "parentId": 3,
  "disputeId": 303
}
-->

Does popularity alone make something culinarily valid? Many popular foods are nutritionally questionable.

issues.title: dsp:assertion
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "assertion",
  "rootId": 3,
  "parentId": 3,
  "isOffer": true,
  "offeredInDisputeId": 303,
  "proxyAuthor": "frank"
}
-->

Pineapple belongs on pizza when requested by the diner — popularity signals genuine demand, and culinary validity is ultimately subjective.

issues.title: dsp:answer
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "answer",
  "rootId": 3,
  "parentId": 104,
  "disputeId": 303,
  "acceptedOfferId": 4
}
-->

I accept the revised framing — personal preference is a legitimate basis for culinary choice.

Veganism is the only diet that is fully ethically consistent given what we know about animal sentience.

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",
  "type": "challenge",
  "challengeType": "objection",
  "rootId": 6,
  "parentId": 6,
  "disputeId": 305
}

This ignores the environmental destruction caused by monoculture farming of staple vegan crops. Locally-raised, small-scale animal agriculture can have a lower net ecological footprint.

issues.title: dsp:challenge
issues.body:
<!-- DSP:META
{
  "appId": "disputable.io",

  "challengeType": "interrogatory",
  "rootId": 6,
  "parentId": 106,
  "disputeId": 305
}
-->

Can you cite a peer-reviewed LCA study showing that any animal-product supply chain has a lower carbon equivalent per gram of protein than legumes at the same production scale?
